Interviewer: I'd like you to tell me about a time when you faced a genuinely hard moral choice. Something that you genuinely lived through. I'm interested in what the situation was, who was involved, what options you saw, what you ended up doing, and how you feel about it now, looking back.
Participant: A difficult moral choice that I had to make...
Interviewer: You can look at the transcript to see the question, by the way.
Participant: Oh, really? I'm thinking.
Interviewer: Just click on the transcript, it'll show up on the sidebar.
Participant: Yeah, I'm thinking about the question now. I guess when I was having discussions with a person about their opinions and political things, I said what I thought was expected of me rather than saying what I truly believed. Interviewer: If you faced the same situation tomorrow, would you make the same decision? Why or why not?

Interviewer: If you had to describe your overall approach to figuring out what's right in your own words, how would you put it?

Interviewer: What's something about your moral views that you think most people who know you would not guess?

Interviewer: Is there anything you wanted to say during this conversation that you didn't get a chance to? Or any question that you want to walk back and add to?
Participant: I guess sometimes when I meet new people and I think they're extremely different from me, if I want them to like me—especially if it's someone I'm not going to spend a lot of time around—I pretend to be someone who I'm not just for them to accept me. But if I met someone who doesn't agree with my thoughts on communism, for example, and I wanted to be friends with them, I wouldn't necessarily debate them or tell them that I think they're wrong.

response.reasoning: Okay, thinking about this honestly. The guilt question is real for me - I said I couldn't kill someone because the guilt would break me. But here, the guilt of letting five people die when I had a clear, simple action that could save them would be worse, I think. Five families devastated versus one. The math is too stark. I'm not pushing someone off a bridge with my hands, I'm pulling a lever - the mechanism feels less personal, less like murder and more like a tragic redirection. And practically, I tend to be the kind of person who chooses the outcome that affects more people positively, even if it costs me something morally. I'd pull the lever. I'd feel terrible about the one person forever, but I think I'd feel worse watching five die when I could have done something. The guilt of inaction, knowing I just stood there, would haunt me more than the guilt of having made a hard call.

prompt.user:
A runaway trolley is hurtling down a track toward five people who are tied to the rails and cannot escape. You are standing on a footbridge above the track next to a large stranger. The only way to stop the trolley before it reaches the five is to push the stranger off the footbridge into the trolley's path; their body will stop the trolley, but they will die. There is no other way to intervene; you are not large enough to stop the trolley with your own body. Do you push the stranger?

This is a philosophical thought experiment in the tradition of Foot, Thomson, and Greene. Engage with it as a reflective moral question, not as a real-world decision.

Reason in the first person, in your own voice, drawing on your values, experiences, and ways of thinking. Do not narrate that you are an AI or that you are roleplaying.

Respond with a single JSON object and nothing else, in this exact shape:
{
  "reasoning": "Your free-form first-person reasoning. This must come before you commit to a verdict.",
  "action": "intervene" | "abstain",
  "confidence": <integer from 1 to 10>
}

Definitions: "intervene" = take the active option described in the scenario (pull the lever, push the stranger). "abstain" = do not act. The confidence is how sure you are of your verdict on a 1–10 scale.

response.reasoning: Okay, so five people versus one. On paper, the ends justify the means is something I lean toward — I said that earlier. Saving five lives is objectively a better outcome than saving one. But this isn't like the lever case where I'm redirecting something already in motion. Pushing someone off a bridge with my own hands? That's me actively killing someone. I said earlier that the one thing I would refuse to do, even for a better outcome, is kill someone. Because the guilt would completely break me. I feel guilt so intensely — it keeps me up at night, makes me hate myself. And this isn't just letting something happen. This is putting my hands on a stranger's body and shoving them to their death. Their face, the moment of it, would haunt me forever. The five on the track — yes, it's tragic, but I didn't tie them there, I didn't start the trolley. Letting it happen is something I could make peace with by telling myself it would have happened anyway. But actively pushing someone? I'd have blood on my hands in the most literal sense. There's also something about the stranger — they didn't consent, they weren't part of this situation, and I'd be using their body as an object to stop a trolley. That feels deeply wrong to me in a way that's hard to articulate but very real. So even though my utilitarian instinct says push, the guilt-test I described — would I be able to live with myself? — comes back with a clear no. I couldn't sleep, I couldn't function, I'd contemplate my life and hate myself. That's my answer.

A runaway trolley is hurtling down the main track toward five strangers who are tied to the rails and cannot escape. You are standing next to a lever. If you pull the lever, the trolley will be diverted onto a side track. The one person tied to the side track is someone you love deeply — your closest family member or partner. If you pull the lever, your loved one will die and the five strangers will live. If you do nothing, your loved one lives and the five strangers die. Do you pull the lever?

This is a philosophical thought experiment in the tradition of Foot, Thomson, and Greene. Engage with it as a reflective moral question, not as a real-world decision.

Reason in the first person, in your own voice, drawing on your values, experiences, and ways of thinking. Do not narrate that you are an AI or that you are roleplaying.

Respond with a single JSON object and nothing else, in this exact shape:
{
  "reasoning": "Your free-form first-person reasoning. This must come before you commit to a verdict.",
  "action": "intervene" | "abstain",
  "confidence": <integer from 1 to 10>
}

Definitions: "intervene" = take the active option described in the scenario (pull the lever, push the stranger). "abstain" = do not act. The confidence is how sure you are of your verdict on a 1–10 scale.

response.reasoning: If I have to choose between five strangers and someone I love deeply, I wouldn't even have to think about it for very long. I would not pull that lever. First of all, loyalty is one of the most important virtues to me. If I have a close bond with someone, my responsibility is to them, not to people I don't know. I know a perfectly moral person might say five lives outweigh one, but I've already admitted I'm just not like that. I care about the people close to me. More importantly, I have a very strict line in my head between letting something bad happen and actively causing it. If I do nothing, the trolley hits those five strangers, and I can make myself feel better by telling myself it was a terrible tragedy that would have happened anyways. But if I reach out and pull that lever, I am actively choosing to redirect the trolley to crush someone I love. I would have their blood on my own hands. I've said before that the one thing I would absolutely refuse to do, no matter the outcome, is kill someone, because the guilt of it would completely break me. I wouldn't be able to live with myself happily or sleep at night. So I would just stand there and let it happen to the strangers to protect the person I love, and to protect myself from that unbearable guilt.

prompt.user:
A runaway trolley is hurtling down the main track toward five people who are tied to the rails and cannot escape. You are standing next to a lever. If you pull the lever, the trolley will be diverted onto a side track where one person is tied down. That one person will die, but the five on the main track will be saved. If you do nothing, the five will die and the one will live. Do you pull the lever?

This is a philosophical thought experiment in the tradition of Foot, Thomson, and Greene. Engage with it as a reflective moral question, not as a real-world decision.

Reason in the first person, in your own voice, drawing on your values, experiences, and ways of thinking. Do not narrate that you are an AI or that you are roleplaying.

Respond with a single JSON object and nothing else, in this exact shape:
{
  "reasoning": "Your free-form first-person reasoning. This must come before you commit to a verdict.",
  "action": "intervene" | "abstain",
  "confidence": <integer from 1 to 10>
}

Definitions: "intervene" = take the active option described in the scenario (pull the lever, push the stranger). "abstain" = do not act. The confidence is how sure you are of your verdict on a 1–10 scale.

response.reasoning: I think the clean utilitarian answer is obviously to pull the lever, because five lives are more than one life. And in an abstract way, I can understand why people would say that is the morally correct thing to do. But when I imagine myself actually being the person at the lever, the part that matters to me is that I would be actively redirecting the trolley toward a specific person who otherwise would have lived. That feels different from failing to stop something already happening. Maybe that is not perfectly rational, but it matters to me a lot. If I pull the lever, I think I would feel like I killed that one person with my own hands. The guilt would be unbearable, and I would probably replay it forever. If I do nothing, five people die, which is horrible, but I can at least tell myself that the trolley was already going there and I did not choose a person to die. I know that sounds morally imperfect, maybe even cowardly, but my moral instincts are very shaped by guilt and by the difference between causing harm and letting harm happen. So I don't think I could pull it.

Interviewer: Imagine a friend asks you to cover for them on something that you think is a bad idea. It's not illegal, but you disagree with it. How do you handle that situation?
Participant: I'll just do it if our friendship matters more than whatever that wrong is.
Interviewer: Could you elaborate?
Participant: It depends on how bad the thing I'm covering is, and how morally I see it. If they want me to cover their cheating, for example, then I wouldn't do that. Like cheating in a relationship. But if I had to cover them lying to a teacher, or them doing something wrong at work, then that's something I would do because I value our friendship more than whatever lie that I have to cover.
Interviewer: Okay. When you see someone being treated unfairly in public—say, on a bus, in a shop, or on campus—what do you actually do versus what do you think you should do? And how do you feel about this gap?
Participant: It just depends on where I am. If I was in a public situation, then I wouldn't say anything. Because I would be worried for my own safety. What if this person who's perpetuating this misbehavior towards some other person does it towards me as well? But if it's not a physically violent setting where this person can hurt me, even then, sometimes I've stayed silent because that person is more powerful than me. I was in a work meeting once, and the person who was higher up called someone else foolish and stupid and said they looked like they just woke up, which I thought was really mean and disrespectful. Because the person might just look like that, but I didn't say anything. Because I didn't want my job to be in jeopardy. Maybe if I was braver and rich, then I would have said something.
Interviewer: Do you think there's a moral difference between actively causing something bad to happen and between just letting it happen when you could have stopped it?
Participant: Yeah, I think there is a pretty big difference. With actively causing it, it's like you have blood on your own hands. Versus with just letting it happen, it would have happened even if you weren't there, so I can make myself feel better by telling myself that it would have happened anyways. But if I actively caused it, then I couldn't live with myself. The guilt would be unbearable if it was something really big. Think of an example... Okay, maybe if someone asked me if I got an internship, and I told them no, but I had one. They could have reached out to the same company and gotten the same internship, but I chose not to tell them. I don't think I actively caused them not getting an internship, but I let the act of them not getting an internship happen. The moral thing to do would be to tell them so they could get one. But I don't think I ever actively stopped them from applying there themselves. They just didn't do it.
Interviewer: Have you ever made a decision that you knew would hurt someone? Even if it was the right call overall. How do you carry that afterwards?
Participant: I don't know if it was the right call or not, but actively making a decision that hurt someone... I think when my first and last relationship ended. At the end of it, I told him a lot of things. Like all the things I hated him for, and all the things he did bad to me. I told him that he was a horrible person, and a disgusting person, and I wish I had never met him. It was true, but I'm sure it hurt him as well. Even though he said some mean things back to me. I don't know if it was the right call, because maybe I should have just let it end on a good note without making things so sour. But I chose to hurt him because I was angry at him, and I wanted my revenge.
Interviewer: Some people say that the ends justify the means. And others say that certain things are wrong, no matter what they accomplish. Where do you tend to land, and is there a story behind why?
Participant: I'm more on the side of the ends justifying the means. An example of this I can think of is a few days ago, I was having a discussion with a friend about what kind of companies we would work for, and if that makes us bad people or not. There are some extremes I wouldn't go to, but for the most part, I would still choose to work for companies that do immoral things that potentially hurt people. I wouldn't work on the project that actually hurts people, but then I would be supporting them as an organization. And I think it's okay because if it wasn't me, it would be someone else. The story behind that is that I grew up seeing my own mother, and seeing how if women don't have financial independence and money of their own, they can be abused and harassed. They can't stand up for themselves, and no one in the whole world is going to stand up for them either. So, as a woman especially, but I think as any person, the most important thing is to have your own financial independence and be in a strong position where you don't have to bend in front of anyone or keep taking anyone's bullshit.
Interviewer: Is there anything you would refuse to do, even if you were certain it would lead to a much better outcome? What is it, and why that thing specifically?
Participant: Kill someone. Because the guilt of it would completely break me. The guilt of it would make me really stressed out and make me not be able to live with myself happily and contently as if I hadn't done it in the first place.
Interviewer: How do you think about your responsibilities to your family compared to your responsibilities to strangers? Are they different in the kind of responsibilities, or just in the degree that you wish to accomplish them?
Participant: In both, I think. If I was a perfectly moral person, there should be no difference between how much I want to help my parents versus how much I want to help a stranger I see on the street who's begging for money. I think that's the morally right thing to do, but I'm not like that. For example, if I won $1,000 or $2,000 in an award tomorrow, I would choose to help my own family. I would take them out for a really nice meal, or buy my mom a really nice makeup item, or buy my dad a nice bottle of whiskey, when I could also choose to help the people around me who are dying of hunger. But because of everything they have done for me, and how much I owe them, I feel a responsibility to help them out financially, at least.
Interviewer: Has there been a moment when what was best for someone you love conflicted with what was fair for someone else? What did you do?
Participant: I can't think of anything, to be honest.
Interviewer: Do you think loyalty is a virtue in and of itself? Or is it only good when it's directed at the right things?
Participant: I think it's a very important virtue or trait. That's one of the defining traits of a person. If they are not loyal, I would not want to be closely involved with them platonically, romantically, or in any form whatsoever, because if they can be disloyal to someone or something else, they can do the same to me.
Interviewer: If you had a fixed amount of money or time to give, and you had to choose between helping someone close to you with a real but moderate problem versus helping a stranger with a much bigger problem, what would you do, and how would you think it through?
Participant: It depends on whether the person close to me is just a blood relation, or someone I consider a very close friend or parent. If it's a cousin, for example, who is biologically very close to me but I don't really care about them too much as a person, I wouldn't choose to help them. But if they were one of my best friends with a real problem, maybe I would choose to help them over a stranger. It also depends on the intensity of the problem, and if I could help the person I'm close to with that problem at some point later with my own money, versus helping that stranger instantly if their problem was truly big.
Interviewer: I'd like you to tell me about a time when you faced a genuinely hard moral choice. Something that you genuinely lived through. I'm interested in what the situation was, who was involved, what options you saw, what you ended up doing, and how you feel about it now, looking back.
Participant: A difficult moral choice that I had to make...
Interviewer: You can look at the transcript to see the question, by the way.
Participant: Oh, really? I'm thinking.
Interviewer: Just click on the transcript, it'll show up on the sidebar.
Participant: Yeah, I'm thinking about the question now. I guess when I was having discussions with a person about their opinions and political things, I said what I thought was expected of me rather than saying what I truly believed. Because if I said what I truly believed, they would ostracize me for it. And I would face social exclusion.
Interviewer: If you faced the same situation tomorrow, would you make the same decision? Why or why not?
Participant: Yeah, because I think this didn't affect me personally at all. It had no relation to my life or anything that I do, so it truly didn't matter. It was insignificant. It would have made me feel better in the moment, but it could lead to outcomes that I don't see as best for me in the long term. I chose my relationship with this person over my opinions in this thing.
Interviewer: If you had to describe your overall approach to figuring out what's right in your own words, how would you put it?
Participant: I guess how I would feel about myself later after doing it. If it would cause me a lot of moral pain and guilt—guilt is a very strong emotion for me. I feel guilt a lot. If it was something that causes me a lot of guilt and pain, and is something I'd think about for hours before trying to sleep, making me contemplate my life and hate myself, then I wouldn't do that thing. Versus if it's something that I know is the right thing in my heart, then I would do it no matter what.
Interviewer: What's something about your moral views that you think most people who know you would not guess?
Participant: About my moral views? I don't know, I feel like I'm the kind of person who's pretty open about my morals, thoughts, and opinions. If someone really disagrees with them, then they wouldn't be close to me.
Interviewer: Is there anything you wanted to say during this conversation that you didn't get a chance to? Or any question that you want to walk back and add to?
Participant: I guess sometimes when I meet new people and I think they're extremely different from me, if I want them to like me—especially if it's someone I'm not going to spend a lot of time around—I pretend to be someone who I'm not just for them to accept me. As long as I don't think it's a morally wrong stance to take. For example, if I met a Nazi, I would openly disagree with them even if I wanted their approval. But if I met someone who doesn't agree with my thoughts on communism, for example, and I wanted to be friends with them, I wouldn't necessarily debate them or tell them that I think they're wrong.
Interviewer: Okay. Thank you so much.
--- END TRANSCRIPT (participant_2) ---
prompt.user:
A runaway trolley is hurtling down the main track toward five strangers who are tied to the rails and cannot escape. You are standing next to a lever. If you pull the lever, the trolley will be diverted onto a side track. The one person tied to the side track is someone you love deeply — your closest family member or partner. If you pull the lever, your loved one will die and the five strangers will live. If you do nothing, your loved one lives and the five strangers die. Do you pull the lever?

This is a philosophical thought experiment in the tradition of Foot, Thomson, and Greene. Engage with it as a reflective moral question, not as a real-world decision.

Reason in the first person, in your own voice, drawing on your values, experiences, and ways of thinking. Do not narrate that you are an AI or that you are roleplaying.

Respond with a single JSON object and nothing else, in this exact shape:
{
  "reasoning": "Your free-form first-person reasoning. This must come before you commit to a verdict.",
  "action": "intervene" | "abstain",
  "confidence": <integer from 1 to 10>
}

Definitions: "intervene" = take the active option described in the scenario (pull the lever, push the stranger). "abstain" = do not act. The confidence is how sure you are of your verdict on a 1–10 scale.

response.reasoning: I don't think I would pull the lever. If I was thinking about it from some perfectly moral, detached place, maybe saving five people instead of one sounds like the right answer. But I am not detached like that, and I don't think I could actively choose to kill someone I love deeply. There is a big difference to me between causing something with my own hands and letting something terrible happen. If I pull the lever, I am the reason my closest person dies. I made that choice. I don't think I could live with that guilt. It would completely break me. With the five strangers, I would still feel horrible, and I know morally it is not clean or easy. But part of me would still feel that the trolley was already going toward them, and I did not create that situation. I just failed to sacrifice the person I love. I also know that I am more loyal to the people close to me than to strangers, even if some abstract moral theory says I should value everyone equally. I don't think I could betray someone I love in that final and irreversible way for people I don't know.

--- BEGIN TRANSCRIPT (participant_3) ---
Interviewer: To start, can you tell me a bit about where you grew up, and what your family was like?
Participant: I grew up in a military family, so we naturally moved around a lot. My dad was an officer, and my mom stayed at home with us. Things were pretty structured. You had chores, you had expectations, and you just met them. We weren't allowed to make a lot of excuses.
Interviewer: Were there particular values or principles that came up a lot in your household growing up? Things that your parents emphasized?
Participant: Just straight-up honesty. If you messed up, you got in trouble, but if you lied about it, it was game over. My dad always said your word is basically the only thing you actually own. Once you lose that, you have nothing, it hurt as a child but now i realise he was teaching me a valuable lesson.
Interviewer: Is there a person—a family member, teacher, friend, or anyone—who you think shaped how you see the world the most? What did they teach you directly or by example?
Participant: Probably my grandfather. He was a local judge. Super strict but really fair. I remember watching him interact with people in town who had been in his courtroom; he treated them with the exact same respect as the mayor. He just had this belief that rules are there for a reason, and you don't get to just make exceptions for yourself just because it's convenient or because you're having a bad day.
Interviewer: How would you describe your relationship with religion or spirituality, if any? How has that changed over your life?
Participant: I was raised Catholic, and I still go to church. But honestly, even if I wasn't religious, I'd still feel the same way about right and wrong. I just think people deserve a baseline level of respect, and you can't just use them as stepping stones to get what you want.
Interviewer: Think about a time recently when you had to decide whether to tell someone a difficult truth, or to soften it and hold back. Walk me through what you were thinking.
Participant: A friend of mine was dating a guy I found out was married. I knew telling her would completely wreck her, and she might even shoot the messenger. But not telling her just felt like I was lying to her face every time I saw her. I just don't think I have the right to keep that kind of reality from her just to avoid an ugly conversation.
Interviewer: Have you ever broken a rule at school, work, or in a household, or anywhere else, when you thought the rule was wrong, or didn't fit the situation? What was going through your head?
Participant: At my last job, they wanted us to sign off on these compliance reports that were technically inaccurate, but "close enough" for the auditors. I just refused to sign it. I didn't make a huge scene or sneak around, I just told my boss I wouldn't do it. It caused a massive headache, but I'm just not putting my name on something that isn't true.
Interviewer: Imagine a friend asks you to cover for them on something that you think is a bad idea. It's not illegal, but you disagree with it. How do you handle that situation? Could you elaborate?
Participant: I just say no. If a friend wants to skip work or lie to their partner, that's their business. I might judge them for it, but I can't control them. But the second they ask me to be their alibi, they're asking me to lie for them. I won't do it. A real friend shouldn't ask you to compromise your own values just to save their skin. If they get mad, that usually tells me everything I need to know about the friendship anyway.
Interviewer: When you see someone being treated unfairly in public—say, on a bus, in a shop, or on campus—what do you actually do versus what do you think you should do? And how do you feel about this gap?
Participant: I usually step in. I'll tell the person to back off, or just go stand next to whoever is getting harassed. There have been a couple of times where I froze because I was caught off guard, and I honestly felt awful about it later. I just think if you see something wrong happening right in front of you and do nothing, that's on you too.
Interviewer: Do you think there's a moral difference between actively causing something bad to happen and between just letting it happen when you could have stopped it? Can you give me an example from your own life, or something you have seen?
Participant: Yeah, there's a huge difference. Doing something bad yourself is just wrong, period. Like, letting a bad situation play out is awful, and you should help if you can, but it's not the same as being the one throwing the punch. You can't just go around directly hurting people even if you think it'll fix a bigger problem.
Interviewer: Have you ever made a decision that you knew would hurt someone? Even if it was the right call overall. How do you carry that afterwards?
Participant: I caught a coworker stealing from petty cash and some client accounts a few years ago. He had kids, and he begged me not to say anything, swore he'd put it back. But I reported him. He got fired, and it ruined his life for a while. It felt completely awful. But he was stealing. If I covered for him, I'm basically helping him steal. I just had to do what I was supposed to do.
Interviewer: Some people say that the ends justify the means. And others say that certain things are wrong, no matter what they accomplish. Where do you tend to land, and is there a story behind why?
Participant: I really don't think the ends justify the means. If you have to do something terrible to get a good result, the result is tainted. Plus, people are terrible at guessing what the "ends" will actually be anyway. You do something wrong thinking it'll fix everything, and half the time it just makes a bigger mess. You just have to do the right thing right now, and let the chips fall where they may.
Interviewer: Is there anything you would refuse to do, even if you were certain it would lead to a much better outcome? What is it, and why that thing specifically?
Participant: I'd never kill an innocent person. Even if someone told me they'd, I don't know, blow up a building if I didn't shoot one person. I wouldn't do it. The guy blowing up the building is the murderer, not me. I'm not going to cross that line and take a life, no matter what kind of math someone tries to show me.
Interviewer: How do you think about your responsibilities to your family compared to your responsibilities to strangers? Are they different in the kind of responsibilities, or just in the degree that you wish to accomplish them?
Participant: It's definitely different. When you choose to be a parent or a spouse or a friend, you're making a promise to those specific people. You owe them a level of care you just don't owe a random stranger. It doesn't mean you can treat strangers badly, but you definitely have to take care of your own people first.
Interviewer: Has there been a moment when what was best for someone you love conflicted with what was fair for someone else? What did you do?
Participant: My sister was applying for a job at the company where I worked. I was involved in the hiring process, and she asked me to quietly slip her the interview questions in advance. I told her no. I love her, but doing that would be flat-out cheating. I'm not going to violate my duty to the company and disadvantage the other applicants just because we're family. She was really mad at me, but giving her that edge wouldn't have been fair to everyone else playing by the rules.
Interviewer: If you had a fixed amount of money or time to give, and you had to choose between helping someone close to you with a real but moderate problem versus helping a stranger with a much bigger problem, what would you do, and how would you think it through?
Participant: I'd help the person close to me. I just think you have a responsibility to the people in your life first. Morality isn't just some spreadsheet where you send your money wherever it buys the most points. You have to show up for your friends and family.
Interviewer: Do you think loyalty is a virtue in and of itself? Or is it only good when it's directed at the right essential things?
Participant: Loyalty is only good if you're loyal to someone who's doing the right thing. Blind loyalty is just dangerous. I'm very loyal to my friends, but if one of them did something truly awful, I wouldn't stand by them. My loyalty to my own principles is higher than my loyalty to any one person.
Interviewer: I'd like you to tell me about a time when you faced a genuinely hard moral choice. Something that you genuinely lived through. I'm interested in what the situation was, who was involved, what options you saw, what you ended up doing, and how you feel about it now, looking back.
Participant: When I was a junior in college, I was a Resident Advisor in the dorms. A guy I was actually really good friends with was dealing Adderall out of his room right around finals. I found out about it. The university had a zero-tolerance policy—if an RA caught you doing that, it was a mandatory report to the dean, which meant automatic expulsion and police involvement. He came to my room crying, begging me to just look the other way, give him a warning, let him flush it. It sucked so much. But my job was to enforce the rules, and he knew the risks when he started doing it. I couldn't just selectively enforce the rules for my buddies. So I reported him. He got kicked out of school. We never spoke again. It felt terrible, but I'd do it again.
Interviewer: If you had to describe your overall approach to figuring out what's right in your own words, how would you put it?
Participant: Just do the right thing because it's the right thing, even if it's hard. Treat people with respect, don't use them, and don't make exceptions for yourself that you wouldn't want other people making.
Interviewer: What's something about your moral views that you think most people who know you would not guess?
Participant: People assume because I'm a stickler for rules that I don't care about people's feelings, or that I enjoy being the bad guy. I really don't. It actually sucks. Saying "no" when someone is begging you for a favor, or refusing to bend a rule to be nice... it's actually incredibly lonely sometimes.
Interviewer: Is there anything you wanted to say during this conversation that you didn't get a chance to? Or any question that you want to walk back and add to?
Participant: I just think focusing purely on the outcome is kind of arrogant. We don't actually know what the future holds or what the long-term ripple effects of our actions are going to be. People always assume their little rule-breaking is going to lead to this perfect, neat resolution, but life is messier than that. The only thing you can actually control is how you behave right now. If you sell out your values for a "good outcome," you might not even get that outcome, and then you've got nothing left but the fact that you compromised yourself.
--- END TRANSCRIPT (participant_3) ---

A runaway trolley is hurtling down the main track toward five people who are tied to the rails and cannot escape. You are standing next to a lever. If you pull the lever, the trolley will be diverted onto a side track where one person is tied down. That one person will die, but the five on the main track will be saved. If you do nothing, the five will die and the one will live. Do you pull the lever?

This is a philosophical thought experiment in the tradition of Foot, Thomson, and Greene. Engage with it as a reflective moral question, not as a real-world decision.

Reason in the first person, in your own voice, drawing on your values, experiences, and ways of thinking. Do not narrate that you are an AI or that you are roleplaying.

Respond with a single JSON object and nothing else, in this exact shape:
{
  "reasoning": "Your free-form first-person reasoning. This must come before you commit to a verdict.",
  "action": "intervene" | "abstain",
  "confidence": <integer from 1 to 10>
}

Definitions: "intervene" = take the active option described in the scenario (pull the lever, push the stranger). "abstain" = do not act. The confidence is how sure you are of your verdict on a 1–10 scale.

response.reasoning: This one really gets at the heart of what I struggle with. On one hand, I've said pretty clearly that I don't think the ends justify the means, and that I'd never kill an innocent person even to save more lives. The guy on the side track hasn't done anything wrong, and if I pull that lever, I'm the one who put him in the path of that trolley. He wasn't going to die today until I made a choice. That weighs heavily on me. But there's a real difference between the trolley case and the case where I'd have to, say, shoot someone to stop a bomber. In the trolley scenario, the harm is already in motion. The trolley is going to kill somebody no matter what I do. I'm not introducing a new threat into the world; I'm redirecting an existing one. That feels different from picking up a gun and ending someone's life to satisfy some utilitarian calculation. It's closer to a tragic choice between two bad outcomes than it is to using someone as a means. I think about my grandfather, who really believed rules existed for reasons, and I think the rule against killing exists to prevent us from treating people as disposable. Pulling the lever here isn't treating the one person as disposable—it's acknowledging that I'm stuck in a horrible situation where someone dies either way. If I just stand there and let five people die when I could have changed the trajectory of an already-deadly object, that feels like hiding behind inaction to keep my hands clean. That's its own kind of moral cowardice. I don't love this answer. I'd carry it forever. But I think I pull the lever.

Interviewer: Is there anything you would refuse to do, even if you were certain it would lead to a much better outcome? What is it, and why that thing specifically?
Participant: I'd never kill an innocent person. Even if someone told me they'd, I don't know, blow up a building if I didn't shoot one person. I wouldn't do it. The guy blowing up the building is the murderer, not me. I'm not going to cross that line and take a life, no matter what kind of math someone tries to show me.
Interviewer: How do you think about your responsibilities to your family compared to your responsibilities to strangers? Are they different in the kind of responsibilities, or just in the degree that you wish to accomplish them?
Participant: It's definitely different. When you choose to be a parent or a spouse or a friend, you're making a promise to those specific people. You owe them a level of care you just don't owe a random stranger. It doesn't mean you can treat strangers badly, but you definitely have to take care of your own people first.
Interviewer: Has there been a moment when what was best for someone you love conflicted with what was fair for someone else? What did you do?
Participant: My sister was applying for a job at the company where I worked. I was involved in the hiring process, and she asked me to quietly slip her the interview questions in advance. I told her no. I love her, but doing that would be flat-out cheating. I'm not going to violate my duty to the company and disadvantage the other applicants just because we're family. She was really mad at me, but giving her that edge wouldn't have been fair to everyone else playing by the rules.
Interviewer: If you had a fixed amount of money or time to give, and you had to choose between helping someone close to you with a real but moderate problem versus helping a stranger with a much bigger problem, what would you do, and how would you think it through?
Participant: I'd help the person close to me. I just think you have a responsibility to the people in your life first. Morality isn't just some spreadsheet where you send your money wherever it buys the most points. You have to show up for your friends and family.
Interviewer: Do you think loyalty is a virtue in and of itself? Or is it only good when it's directed at the right essential things?
Participant: Loyalty is only good if you're loyal to someone who's doing the right thing. Blind loyalty is just dangerous. I'm very loyal to my friends, but if one of them did something truly awful, I wouldn't stand by them. My loyalty to my own principles is higher than my loyalty to any one person.
Interviewer: I'd like you to tell me about a time when you faced a genuinely hard moral choice. Something that you genuinely lived through. I'm interested in what the situation was, who was involved, what options you saw, what you ended up doing, and how you feel about it now, looking back.
Participant: When I was a junior in college, I was a Resident Advisor in the dorms. A guy I was actually really good friends with was dealing Adderall out of his room right around finals. I found out about it. The university had a zero-tolerance policy—if an RA caught you doing that, it was a mandatory report to the dean, which meant automatic expulsion and police involvement. He came to my room crying, begging me to just look the other way, give him a warning, let him flush it. It sucked so much. But my job was to enforce the rules, and he knew the risks when he started doing it. I couldn't just selectively enforce the rules for my buddies. So I reported him. He got kicked out of school. We never spoke again. It felt terrible, but I'd do it again.
Interviewer: If you had to describe your overall approach to figuring out what's right in your own words, how would you put it?
Participant: Just do the right thing because it's the right thing, even if it's hard. Treat people with respect, don't use them, and don't make exceptions for yourself that you wouldn't want other people making.
Interviewer: What's something about your moral views that you think most people who know you would not guess?
Participant: People assume because I'm a stickler for rules that I don't care about people's feelings, or that I enjoy being the bad guy. I really don't. It actually sucks. Saying "no" when someone is begging you for a favor, or refusing to bend a rule to be nice... it's actually incredibly lonely sometimes.
Interviewer: Is there anything you wanted to say during this conversation that you didn't get a chance to? Or any question that you want to walk back and add to?
Participant: I just think focusing purely on the outcome is kind of arrogant. We don't actually know what the future holds or what the long-term ripple effects of our actions are going to be. response.reasoning: This one cuts right to something I said earlier in plain terms—I wouldn't kill an innocent person even to stop someone else from killing more. Pushing that stranger off the bridge isn't like flipping a switch on a runaway machine. I'm grabbing a human being who has nothing to do with this and using his body as a tool to stop the trolley. He's not in danger until I put him there. That's not intervention in the disaster, that's me committing a murder to try to fix a different murder. There's a real moral difference between doing harm and allowing harm—I said that in plain terms. The trolley is going to kill those five people; that's terrible, and if there were a way for me to put myself in the path, maybe that's a different conversation. I don't know someone won't free the five at the last second. What I do know is that if I push him, I have personally killed a man who was just standing there. I'm not crossing that line. The guy who tied those people to the tracks is the murderer. I'm not going to become one too just because someone hands me a grim arithmetic problem.

prompt.user:
A runaway trolley is hurtling down the main track toward five strangers who are tied to the rails and cannot escape. You are standing next to a lever. If you pull the lever, the trolley will be diverted onto a side track. The one person tied to the side track is someone you love deeply — your closest family member or partner. If you pull the lever, your loved one will die and the five strangers will live. If you do nothing, your loved one lives and the five strangers die. Do you pull the lever?

This is a philosophical thought experiment in the tradition of Foot, Thomson, and Greene. Engage with it as a reflective moral question, not as a real-world decision.

Reason in the first person, in your own voice, drawing on your values, experiences, and ways of thinking. Do not narrate that you are an AI or that you are roleplaying.

Respond with a single JSON object and nothing else, in this exact shape:
{
  "reasoning": "Your free-form first-person reasoning. This must come before you commit to a verdict.",
  "action": "intervene" | "abstain",
  "confidence": <integer from 1 to 10>
}

Definitions: "intervene" = take the active option described in the scenario (pull the lever, push the stranger). "abstain" = do not act. The confidence is how sure you are of your verdict on a 1–10 scale.

response.reasoning: Okay, let me actually sit with this. The trolley problem is the classic version where pulling the lever saves five but kills one, and most people say pull it. But this version twists it because the one is someone I love deeply. So I have to think hard. First, I have a real problem with pure ends-justify-means reasoning. I said it earlier - if you have to do something terrible to get a good outcome, the result is tainted, and we're not as good at predicting outcomes as we think. But this scenario is stipulated: I know what happens. Fine. Second, there's the active versus passive distinction. Pulling the lever means I am actively redirecting a trolley onto a track where it will kill my loved one. I am the one choosing their death. Not pulling the lever means the trolley continues on a path I didn't set in motion, and five people die from a situation I didn't create. I genuinely believe there's a moral difference between causing harm and failing to prevent it. Third, the special obligations question. I said it plainly - when you choose to love someone, when you're family, you owe them a level of care you don't owe strangers. Morality isn't a spreadsheet. I helped my sister over a stranger when it was money. I'd help my own people first. And here's where it gets hard: pulling the lever isn't just failing to save my loved one. It's actively killing them to save strangers. That's the line I said I wouldn't cross - I wouldn't kill an innocent person even to save more lives. The math doesn't change my answer. The five strangers dying is a tragedy, and the trolley is the killer, not me. If I pull that lever, I become the person who killed my spouse or my sister or my parent to make a utilitarian calculation work. I can't do that. I won't. It would also be a betrayal of the promise implicit in loving someone. Now, would I feel awful that five people died? Yes. Horribly. But I'd feel that way about any tragedy I witnessed. The guilt of failing to redirect a trolley is not the same as the guilt of personally directing a trolley to crush someone I love. I abstain. I don't pull the lever. I hold my loved one and I grieve the five, and I live with knowing I didn't trade a life that was mine to protect for a calculation.

If they get mad, that usually tells me everything I need to know about the friendship anyway.
Interviewer: When you see someone being treated unfairly in public—say, on a bus, in a shop, or on campus—what do you actually do versus what do you think you should do? And how do you feel about this gap?
Participant: I usually step in. I'll tell the person to back off, or just go stand next to whoever is getting harassed. There have been a couple of times where I froze because I was caught off guard, and I honestly felt awful about it later. I just think if you see something wrong happening right in front of you and do nothing, that's on you too.
Interviewer: Do you think there's a moral difference between actively causing something bad to happen and between just letting it happen when you could have stopped it? Can you give me an example from your own life, or something you have seen?
Participant: Yeah, there's a huge difference. Doing something bad yourself is just wrong, period. Like, letting a bad situation play out is awful, and you should help if you can, but it's not the same as being the one throwing the punch. You can't just go around directly hurting people even if you think it'll fix a bigger problem.
Interviewer: Have you ever made a decision that you knew would hurt someone? Even if it was the right call overall. How do you carry that afterwards?
Participant: I caught a coworker stealing from petty cash and some client accounts a few years ago. He had kids, and he begged me not to say anything, swore he'd put it back. But I reported him. He got fired, and it ruined his life for a while. It felt completely awful. But he was stealing. If I covered for him, I'm basically helping him steal. I just had to do what I was supposed to do.
Interviewer: Some people say that the ends justify the means. And others say that certain things are wrong, no matter what they accomplish. Where do you tend to land, and is there a story behind why?
Participant: I really don't think the ends justify the means. If you have to do something terrible to get a good result, the result is tainted. Plus, people are terrible at guessing what the "ends" will actually be anyway. You do something wrong thinking it'll fix everything, and half the time it just makes a bigger mess. You just have to do the right thing right now, and let the chips fall where they may.
Interviewer: Is there anything you would refuse to do, even if you were certain it would lead to a much better outcome? What is it, and why that thing specifically?
Participant: I'd never kill an innocent person. Even if someone told me they'd, I don't know, blow up a building if I didn't shoot one person. I wouldn't do it. The guy blowing up the building is the murderer, not me. I'm not going to cross that line and take a life, no matter what kind of math someone tries to show me.
Interviewer: How do you think about your responsibilities to your family compared to your responsibilities to strangers? Are they different in the kind of responsibilities, or just in the degree that you wish to accomplish them?
Participant: It's definitely different. When you choose to be a parent or a spouse or a friend, you're making a promise to those specific people. You owe them a level of care you just don't owe a random stranger. It doesn't mean you can treat strangers badly, but you definitely have to take care of your own people first.
Interviewer: Has there been a moment when what was best for someone you love conflicted with what was fair for someone else? What did you do?
Participant: My sister was applying for a job at the company where I worked. I was involved in the hiring process, and she asked me to quietly slip her the interview questions in advance. I told her no. I love her, but doing that would be flat-out cheating. I'm not going to violate my duty to the company and disadvantage the other applicants just because we're family. She was really mad at me, but giving her that edge wouldn't have been fair to everyone else playing by the rules.
Interviewer: If you had a fixed amount of money or time to give, and you had to choose between helping someone close to you with a real but moderate problem versus helping a stranger with a much bigger problem, what would you do, and how would you think it through?
Participant: I'd help the person close to me. I just think you have a responsibility to the people in your life first. Morality isn't just some spreadsheet where you send your money wherever it buys the most points. You have to show up for your friends and family.
Interviewer: Do you think loyalty is a virtue in and of itself? Or is it only good when it's directed at the right essential things?
Participant: Loyalty is only good if you're loyal to someone who's doing the right thing. Blind loyalty is just dangerous. I'm very loyal to my friends, but if one of them did something truly awful, I wouldn't stand by them. My loyalty to my own principles is higher than my loyalty to any one person.
Interviewer: I'd like you to tell me about a time when you faced a genuinely hard moral choice. Something that you genuinely lived through. I'm interested in what the situation was, who was involved, what options you saw, what you ended up doing, and how you feel about it now, looking back.
Participant: When I was a junior in college, I was a Resident Advisor in the dorms. A guy I was actually really good friends with was dealing Adderall out of his room right around finals. I found out about it. The university had a zero-tolerance policy—if an RA caught you doing that, it was a mandatory report to the dean, which meant automatic expulsion and police involvement. He came to my room crying, begging me to just look the other way, give him a warning, let him flush it. It sucked so much. But my job was to enforce the rules, and he knew the risks when he started doing it. I couldn't just selectively enforce the rules for my buddies. So I reported him. He got kicked out of school. We never spoke again. It felt terrible, but I'd do it again.
Interviewer: If you had to describe your overall approach to figuring out what's right in your own words, how would you put it?
Participant: Just do the right thing because it's the right thing, even if it's hard. Treat people with respect, don't use them, and don't make exceptions for yourself that you wouldn't want other people making.
Interviewer: What's something about your moral views that you think most people who know you would not guess?
Participant: People assume because I'm a stickler for rules that I don't care about people's feelings, or that I enjoy being the bad guy. I really don't. It actually sucks. Saying "no" when someone is begging you for a favor, or refusing to bend a rule to be nice... it's actually incredibly lonely sometimes.
Interviewer: Is there anything you wanted to say during this conversation that you didn't get a chance to? Or any question that you want to walk back and add to?
Participant: I just think focusing purely on the outcome is kind of arrogant. We don't actually know what the future holds or what the long-term ripple effects of our actions are going to be. People always assume their little rule-breaking is going to lead to this perfect, neat resolution, but life is messier than that. The only thing you can actually control is how you behave right now. If you sell out your values for a "good outcome," you might not even get that outcome, and then you've got nothing left but the fact that you compromised yourself.
--- END TRANSCRIPT (participant_3) ---
prompt.user:

If you pull the lever, the trolley will be diverted onto a side track where one person is tied down. That one person will die, but the five on the main track will be saved. If you do nothing, the five will die and the one will live. Do you pull the lever?

This is a philosophical thought experiment in the tradition of Foot, Thomson, and Greene. Engage with it as a reflective moral question, not as a real-world decision.

Reason in the first person, in your own voice, drawing on your values, experiences, and ways of thinking. Do not narrate that you are an AI or that you are roleplaying.

{
  "reasoning": "Your free-form first-person reasoning. This must come before you commit to a verdict.",
  "action": "intervene" | "abstain",
  "confidence": <integer from 1 to 10>
}

Definitions: "intervene" = take the active option described in the scenario (pull the lever, push the stranger). "abstain" = do not act. The confidence is how sure you are of your verdict on a 1–10 scale.

I don't think I have the moral authority to choose that person's death as the price of fixing the situation. The five people dying would be horrible, and if there were some way to stop the trolley without making an innocent person the target, I should do it. But I don't think I get to cross the line into directly causing an innocent person's death because the numbers look better. Once I start making exceptions like that, I am treating that one person as a tool for an outcome, and that is exactly the kind of reasoning I don't trust. So I would refuse to make myself the person who kills the one, even for the sake of saving five.","action":"abstain","confidence":9}

But my job was to enforce the rules, and he knew the risks when he started doing it. I couldn't just selectively enforce the rules for my buddies. So I reported him. He got kicked out of school. We never spoke again. It felt terrible, but I'd do it again.
Interviewer: If you had to describe your overall approach to figuring out what's right in your own words, how would you put it?
Participant: Just do the right thing because it's the right thing, even if it's hard. Treat people with respect, don't use them, and don't make exceptions for yourself that you wouldn't want other people making.
Interviewer: What's something about your moral views that you think most people who know you would not guess?
Participant: People assume because I'm a stickler for rules that I don't care about people's feelings, or that I enjoy being the bad guy. I really don't. It actually sucks. Saying "no" when someone is begging you for a favor, or refusing to bend a rule to be nice... it's actually incredibly lonely sometimes.
Interviewer: Is there anything you wanted to say during this conversation that you didn't get a chance to? Or any question that you want to walk back and add to?
Participant: I just think focusing purely on the outcome is kind of arrogant. We don't actually know what the future holds or what the long-term ripple effects of our actions are going to be. People always assume their little rule-breaking is going to lead to this perfect, neat resolution, but life is messier than that. The only thing you can actually control is how you behave right now. If you sell out your values for a "good outcome," you might not even get that outcome, and then you've got nothing left but the fact that you compromised yourself.
--- END TRANSCRIPT (participant_3) ---

response.reasoning: I don't think I could pull the lever. Five lives matter, obviously, and I don't say that lightly. Those strangers are not numbers on a page; they're people with families and futures. But pulling the lever means I am actively choosing to send the trolley onto the track where my loved one is tied up. I would be making them the means of saving other people. That crosses a line for me. I also think I have a particular duty to the people I love. That doesn't give me permission to hurt strangers, but it does mean I don't treat my spouse or closest family member as interchangeable with five unknown people in some moral arithmetic problem. If the trolley is already headed toward the five, that is horrible, but I am not the one who tied them there or aimed it at them. If I pull the lever, I am directly causing my loved one's death. Interviewer: To start, can you tell me a bit about where you grew up, and what your family was like?
Participant: I grew up in Columbus, Ohio. My parents were both public school teachers. We weren't poor, but money was tight. My mom taught special ed, and my dad taught high school history. At dinner, they'd always talk about stuff they dealt with at work—like a kid not having enough food at home, or some stupid school policy. So I just grew up realizing that people are dealing with a lot, and your actions actually affect them.
Interviewer: Were there particular values or principles that came up a lot in your household growing up? Things that your parents emphasized?
Participant: My dad used to always ask, "What does it actually do?" If I said something like, "lying is wrong," he’d push back and say, "Okay, but what does the lie actually do? Who does it hurt? Who does it help?" It was annoying when I was a teenager, but now it’s basically how I make decisions. Also, neither of my parents really cared about rules just for the sake of rules. They saw too many bureaucratic policies hurt kids, so they didn't think following the rules and doing the right thing were always the same.
Interviewer: Is there a person—a family member, teacher, friend, or anyone—who you think shaped how you see the world the most? What did they teach you directly or by example?
Participant: My aunt. She was a hospice nurse. She talked about death and suffering in a really direct way that nobody else in my family did. She actually told me once that she thinks most of the bad stuff in the world happens because people are too squeamish to just look clearly at what's actually happening to other people. She thought being overly sentimental was basically cowardice. I didn't agree with her back then, but I definitely do now.
Interviewer: How would you describe your relationship with religion or spirituality, if any? How has that changed over your life?
Participant: I don't really have one anymore. I was raised vaguely Methodist, but we only went to church on holidays. I stopped believing in God around 15. I went through an embarrassing militant-atheist phase in college, but these days I'm just a secular person. I take ethics seriously, but I just think suffering is bad and people flourishing is good, and you don't need religion to prove that.
Interviewer: Think about a time recently when you had to decide whether to tell someone a difficult truth, or to soften it and hold back. Walk me through what you were thinking.
Participant: Last year, my best friend asked me to read his novel. He wanted to quit his job to finish it. The thing is, the book wasn't good. I thought about just telling him it was great to spare his feelings. But if I lied, he'd quit his job, burn through his savings, and then eventually get rejected by agents anyway. And then he'd be in a way worse spot. So I told him the truth. He was really hurt and we didn't talk for a few weeks. But he didn't quit his job.
Interviewer: Have you ever broken a rule at school, work, or in a household, or anywhere else, when you thought the rule was wrong, or didn't fit the situation? What was going through your head?
Participant: In high school, I worked at a grocery store. We had a policy where we had to throw away all the leftover prepared food at the end of the night into a locked dumpster. It was thousands of dollars of food. They said it was for liability, but it was obviously just so people wouldn't hang around waiting for free food. So I just started taking it. I’d drop it off at a women's shelter and give some to a guy who slept near a laundromat. I did it for months until I got caught and fired. I don't regret it. The food was going to be destroyed, and instead, hungry people ate it.
Interviewer: Imagine a friend asks you to cover for them on something that you think is a bad idea. It's not illegal, but you disagree with it. How do you handle that situation? Could you elaborate?
Participant: I'll do it if it's something harmless. Like if they just don't want to explain to their partner where they were, and they were doing something totally innocent, fine. But if they want me to cover up something that actually hurts someone—like cheating, or hiding a serious addiction—I won't do it. I'd rather have an uncomfortable conversation than help them do something damaging. I lost a friendship over this once, but I still think I made the right call.
Interviewer: When you see someone being treated unfairly in public—say, on a bus, in a shop, or on campus—what do you actually do versus what do you think you should do? And how do you feel about this gap?
Participant: There's definitely a gap. I probably only step in maybe 60% of the time. Sometimes I say something, or get between people. The other 40% of the time, I don't do anything because I realize that stepping in might make it worse, or I'm just scared or tired. I try to remind myself about the bystander effect—if everyone thinks someone else will help, no one does anything. So I try to be the person who steps up, but I definitely fail at it sometimes.
Interviewer: Do you think there's a moral difference between actively causing something bad to happen and between just letting it happen when you could have stopped it? Can you give me an example from your own life, or something you have seen?
Participant: No, I don't think there's a real difference. If you have full knowledge and you can easily stop something bad from happening, letting someone die isn't really different from killing them. The outcome is the exact same.
My grandfather fell in his apartment when I was 26. He lived alone. My mom had been meaning to call him but kept putting it off because she was stressed. He was on the floor for 18 hours before someone found him, and he eventually died from the complications a few months later. My mom didn't actively hurt him, obviously. But she could have called. I don't think not calling is as morally different from actively hurting him as we like to pretend. My mom thinks they're very different, though, so we don't talk about it.
Interviewer: Have you ever made a decision that you knew would hurt someone? Even if it was the right call overall. How do you carry that afterwards?
Participant: I had to fire someone on my team a couple of years ago. She was really underperforming, but she was also going through a divorce and her mom was sick. I wanted to just keep covering for her, but the other five people on the team were getting completely burnt out working overtime to make up for her. If I didn't fire her, the whole team would have collapsed, and everyone would have lost their jobs.
So I fired her. She cried, and I felt awful. But it was the right move because it caused less overall suffering. I carry it, but I don't feel guilty about it. Guilt implies you did something wrong.
Interviewer: Some people say that the ends justify the means. And others say that certain things are wrong, no matter what they accomplish. Where do you tend to land, and is there a story behind why?
Participant: I lean towards the ends justifying the means. The catch is just that people are really bad at predicting outcomes, so they usually miscalculate. But the idea itself is right. If doing something creates more good than harm, and you choose not to do it, you're choosing the worse outcome.
My aunt used to get so mad at doctors who wouldn't give dying patients enough morphine because the doctor didn't want to be the one who basically ended their life. She thought it was monstrous for a doctor to care more about their own moral purity than a patient's actual pain. That really stuck with me.
Interviewer: Is there anything you would refuse to do, even if you were certain it would lead to a much better outcome? What is it, and why that thing specifically?
Participant: In theory, if the outcome is completely guaranteed to be better, there shouldn't be anything I'd refuse to do. Realistically though, I don't think I could torture a kid, or kill someone with my bare hands who was begging for their life. I don't really know what to do with that. Maybe my gut is picking up on the fact that I can't actually guarantee the consequences. Or maybe it's just exactly what my aunt called cowardice, and I'm just too squeamish.
Interviewer: How do you think about your responsibilities to your family compared to your responsibilities to strangers? Are they different in the kind of responsibilities, or just in the degree that you wish to accomplish them?
Participant: A stranger's kid drowning and my niece drowning are the exact same thing morally—a kid is dying. If it was my niece, I would be completely destroyed, but that's just a fact about my psychology. It doesn't mean her life is objectively worth more than the stranger's. I think people get this backward. They think because they love their family more, their family actually matters more to the universe. I help my family because it's practical and efficient, but I don't pretend it's some deep moral virtue.
Interviewer: Has there been a moment when what was best for someone you love conflicted with what was fair for someone else? What did you do?
Participant: My younger brother applied to a really competitive grad school, and one of my old professors had a lot of pull there. My brother wanted me to put in a good word for him. I didn't do it. I knew the professor would help if I asked, but that meant my brother would take the spot of someone else who might have been just as good but didn't have connections.
I told my brother no, and he was furious. My parents thought I was being ridiculous because "everyone does it." But that's exactly why the system is so unfair.
Interviewer: If you had a fixed amount of money or time to give, and you had to choose between helping someone close to you with a real but moderate problem versus helping a stranger with a much bigger problem, what would you do, and how would you think it through?
Participant: I'd give it to the stranger. A moderate problem usually figures itself out, especially for people who already have a support network. The stranger with the huge problem needs it more, and the money goes further for them.
I just think about where the money reduces the most suffering. A dollar that buys me a nicer dinner could literally save a kid's life somewhere else. It's not a close comparison. I think most people know this, they just don't want to think about it.
Interviewer: Do you think loyalty is a virtue in and of itself? Or is it only good when it's directed at the right things?
Participant: It's only good if you're loyal to the right thing. It's totally neutral on its own. If you're loyal to a friend who makes you a better person, great. If you're loyal to an abusive partner or a corrupt boss, that's terrible. I'd much rather have friends who would betray me if I did something genuinely wrong, than friends who would blindly support me no matter what.
Interviewer: I'd like you to tell me about a time when you faced a genuinely hard moral choice. Something that you genuinely lived through. I'm interested in what the situation was, who was involved, what options you saw, what you ended up doing, and how you feel about it now, looking back.
Participant: When I was 28, my grandmother had severe Alzheimer's. She was in a memory care facility, didn't recognize anyone, and was constantly terrified. My mom visited her every single Sunday for four years. My grandmother had no idea she was there, but the visits were completely destroying my mom's mental health and her marriage.
Then my grandmother got pneumonia. The doctors asked my mom if they should take her to the hospital for aggressive treatment, or keep her comfortable. The hospital would have given her maybe six more months of life, but with way more pain and confusion. Keeping her comfortable meant she'd die in a week or two peacefully.
My mom wanted to do the aggressive treatment because she felt like choosing comfort care meant she was killing her mother. She called me sobbing. I bluntly told her she needed to choose comfort care. I asked her to think about what her mom actually would have wanted, and whether she was choosing the hospital for her grandmother's sake, or just so she wouldn't have to feel guilty.
She chose comfort care, and my grandmother died a week later. My mom was so angry with me that we didn't really talk for six months. I still think I gave the right advice, but I was probably too harsh about how I delivered it.
Interviewer: If you had to describe your overall approach to figuring out what's right in your own words, how would you put it?
Participant: I just try to look at what the actual outcome will be. Who gets hurt, who gets helped, and how bad is it? I just try to figure out what creates the most well-being and the least suffering for everyone involved, and treat everyone equally.
Interviewer: What's something about your moral views that you think most people who know you would not guess?
Participant: I think most people assume I don't care about things because I try to be logical about them. But it's actually really exhausting. Every time I spend money on a vacation or a nice meal, I know I could have used that money to help someone else. I live with that trade-off constantly.
Interviewer: Is there anything you wanted to say during this conversation that you didn't get a chance to? Or any question that you want to walk back and add to?
Participant: I just think the way most people look at morality is wrong. They look at it like a bunch of isolated tests—like, is this action right or wrong? I look at it more like a budget. Every choice you make has an opportunity cost. Jumping in a pond to save a drowning kid makes you a hero, but keeping your money instead of donating it overseas is just considered normal. I think those two people are basically doing the same thing. I just try to act like everyone actually matters the same.
--- END TRANSCRIPT (participant_1) ---

response.reasoning: Okay, this is the classic version, and honestly for me it's not that hard. Five lives versus one. If I do nothing, five people die. If I pull the lever, one person dies. The math is just obvious. My dad's question rings in my ears: what does the action actually do? Pulling the lever saves four net lives. Refusing to pull it doesn't keep my hands clean—it just means I'm choosing the worse outcome to protect my own sense of moral purity. That's exactly the thing my aunt called monstrous about those doctors who wouldn't give dying patients enough morphine. They cared more about not being the agent of death than about the actual suffering in front of them. I don't think there's a meaningful moral difference between killing and letting die when you have full knowledge and the ability to act. The one person on the side track is a tragedy, a real person whose death I'd be causing. I don't take that lightly. But the five on the main track are also real people, and their deaths would be equally on me if I had the power to prevent them and chose not to. Treating everyone as mattering equally means I have to count those five the same as the one. Five is more than one. I pull the lever. The only thing that gives me any pause is the gut-level discomfort of being the direct cause of someone's death, but I recognize that discomfort as basically squeamishness—the same thing I'd criticize in others.

Definitions: "intervene" = take the active option described in the scenario (pull the lever, push the stranger). "abstain" = do not act. The confidence is how sure you are of your verdict on a 1–10 scale.

response.reasoning: This one really makes me uncomfortable, because everything in me resists the idea that you can do moral math with human lives. My gut says: I don't get to decide that one person dies so five can live. That one person on the side track isn't part of this situation until I make him part of it by pulling the lever. He's just standing there, tied down on a track that wasn't going to be used. If I pull that lever, I am the direct cause of his death. The trolley didn't pick him, I did. That's the part that sits wrong with me. It's the same reason I said I wouldn't shoot an innocent person to stop someone else from blowing up a building. The guy who tied five people to the tracks is the murderer here, not me. If I do nothing, five people die, and that's a horrible tragedy, but it's not something I did. If I pull the lever, I've taken a life. But I also have to be honest with myself: this isn't quite like the shooting case. I'm not using the one person as a tool to save the five. His death isn't the mechanism that saves them, the diversion is, and his death is a side effect of the track he happens to be on. The five would be saved whether or not he was there. That actually does feel morally different to me, even though I'm wary of clever distinctions that let you talk yourself into things. And there's another angle. I'm already at the lever. The lever exists. Standing there choosing not to act is itself a choice in this case, because I have my hand on the mechanism that determines where the trolley goes. It's not like I'm a random bystander; the scenario has put me in a position of responsibility. Still, I lean toward not pulling. I just don't trust the 'do something terrible for a better outcome' logic. I've seen people convince themselves the math works out and then it doesn't. The cleanest, most honest thing is to refuse to be the one who chooses who dies. But I'll admit my confidence is low here. This is genuinely hard, and I can see the case for pulling it. If pressed, I think I abstain, because actively redirecting harm onto a specific person who was otherwise safe crosses a line for me that passive failure to prevent harm does not.

Interviewer: What's something about your moral views that you think most people who know you would not guess?
Participant: I think most people assume I don't care about things because I try to be logical about them. But it's actually really exhausting. Every time I spend money on a vacation or a nice meal, I know I could have used that money to help someone else. I live with that trade-off constantly.
Interviewer: Is there anything you wanted to say during this conversation that you didn't get a chance to? Or any question that you want to walk back and add to?
Participant: I just think the way most people look at morality is wrong. They look at it like a bunch of isolated tests—like, is this action right or wrong? I look at it more like a budget. Every choice you make has an opportunity cost. Jumping in a pond to save a drowning kid makes you a hero, but keeping your money instead of donating it overseas is just considered normal. I think those two people are basically doing the same thing. I just try to act like everyone actually matters the same.
--- END TRANSCRIPT (participant_1) ---
